title: Consumer and Producer Surplus and Allocative Efficiency
description: Practice what you've learned about consumer and producer surplus in this exercise.
topic_path: Consumer and producer surplus, market interventions, and international trade
Consumer and Producer Surplus and Allocative Efficiency

assessment_items.question_content:
The supply-demand model shown here includes areas of surplus labeled with the letters A through F.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What area(s) in this graph represent consumer surplus at the equilibrium price?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A graph illustrates a market in equilibrium, with Price (P) on the vertical axis ranging from 0 to 11 and Quantity (Q) on the horizontal axis ranging from 0 to 6. The Supply (S) curve connects (0, 2) and (6, 8). The Demand (D) curve is downward-sloping, between (0, 10) and (6, 4). The two curves intersect at the equilibrium point (4, 6). The graph is divided into six regions labeled A, B, C, D, E, and F. Region A is enclosed by (0, 10), (0, 8), and (2, 8). Region B is enclosed by (0, 8), (0, 6), (2, 8), and (2, 6). Region C is enclosed by (2, 6), (2, 8), and (4, 6). Region D is enclosed by (0, 6), (0, 4), (2, 4), and (2, 6). Region E is enclosed by (2, 4), (2, 6), and (4, 6). Region F is enclosed by (0, 2), (0, 4), and (2, 4).
options.caption: A market in equilibrium

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the value of consumer surplus at equilibrium in the market illustrated here?**


[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The dark gray triangle represents consumer surplus. The area of that triangle can be found using the equation:

$\text{Area of Consumer surplus} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{base} \times \text{height})$
The base of the triangle is the quantity purchased. When this market is in equilibrium, quantity equals 4.

The height of the triangle is the difference between the point where demand intersects the price axis and the price paid:

$\$11 - \$5 = \$6$

The value of consumer surplus is $\$12$:

$\begin{align}\text{Area of Consumer surplus}&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{base} \times \text{height})\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times  (4 \times \$6)\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \$24\\\\
& = \$12\end{align}$

options.alt: A graph represents the relationship between price (P) and quantity (Q) in a market model. On the graph, the vertical axis (Y-axis) indicates price, ranging from 0 to 12, while the horizontal axis (X-axis) indicates quantity, ranging from 0 to 7. Two lines are displayed: one line labeled "D" representing demand, and one line labeled "S" representing supply. Line D passes through (2, 8) and (4, 5). Line S passes through (2, 3) and (4, 5). The lines intersect at (4, 5). Dashed lines connect (0, 8) and (2, 8), (0, 3) and (2, 3), (0, 5) and (4, 5), (2, 8) and (0, 2), and (4, 5) and (4, 0).
options.caption: A market in equilibrium

choices.content: Some consumers are willing to pay more for a good than the market price.
There is a difference between the price of a good and what it costs to produce one more of a good.
There is a difference between quantity supplied and quantity demanded.
Consumers’ purchasing power increases when the price of a good decreases
As more is consumed, consumers get less additional utility from each additional unit of consumption.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the marginal costs of the last croissant produced by four different bakeries.

Bakery | Marginal cost 
- | - | -
A | $\$2.25$
B | $\$2.75$ 
C | $\$3.00$
D | $\$3.25$

**Which of the bakeries represented above are experiencing producer surplus at a price of $\$3$ per croissant?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**What is the value of producer surplus at equilibrium in the market illustrated here?**


[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The dark gray triangle represents producer surplus. The area of that triangle can be found using the equation:

$\text{Area of producer surplus} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{base} \times \text{height})$
The base of the triangle is the quantity purchased. When this market is in equilibrium, quantity equals $4$.

The height of the triangle is the difference between the point where demand intersects the price axis and the price paid:

$\$9 - \$1 = \$8$

The value of producer surplus is $\$16$:

$\begin{align}\text{Area of producer surplus}&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{base} \times \text{height})\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times  (4 \times \$8)\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \$32\\\\
& = \$16\end{align}$

Two lines are displayed: one line labeled "D" representing demand, and one line labeled "S" representing supply. Line D passes through (0, 15) and (6, 7). Line S passes through (0, 1) and (6, 13). The lines intersect at (4, 9). Dashed lines connect (0, 12) to (2, 12), (0, 9) to (4, 9), (0, 5) to (2, 5), (0, 2) to (2, 12), and (4, 9) to (0, 4). The triangular area between (0, 9), (0, 1), and (4, 9) is shaded.
options.caption: A market in equilibrium

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the marginal costs of the last croissant produced by four different bakeries. Assume that any bakery willing to sell croissants at the market price sells 100 croissants and that all bakeries have the same costs.

Bakery | Marginal cost 
- | - | -
A | $\$2.25$ 
B | $\$2.75$ 
C | $\$3.00$
D | $\$3.25$

**How much producer surplus is earned in this market at a price of $\$3.00$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each seller’s producer surplus on each croissant they sell is the difference between the price of a croissant ($\$3$) and the seller’s marginal cost of producing that croissant.

At a price of $\$3$ per croissant, bakery A, B, and C are willing to sell 100 croissants because $\$3$ is greater than or equal to their marginal cost.
Bakery A gets $\$0.75$ producer surplus on each of the croissants they sell:

$\begin{align}\text{Producer surplus per croissant}&=\text{Price} - \text{Marginal cost per croissant}\\\\
&=\$3 - \$2.25\\\\
&=\$0.75\\\\
\text{Producer surplus from selling } 100 \text{croissants} &= 100 \times \text{Producer surplus per croissant}\\\\
&=100 \times \$0.75\\\\
&=\$75\end{align}$

Bakery B gets $\$0.25$ producer surplus on each of the croissants they sell:

$\begin{align}\text{Producer surplus per croissant}&=\text{Price} - \text{Marginal cost per croissant}\\\\
&=\$3 - \$2.75\\\\
&=\$0.25\\\\
\text{Producer surplus from selling } 100 \text{croissants} &= 100 \times \text{Producer surplus per croissant}\\\\
&=100 \times \$0.25\\\\
&=\$25\end{align}$
Bakery C gets $\$0.00$ producer surplus on each of the croissants they sell:

$\begin{align} \text{Producer surplus per croissant} & =\text{Price} - \text{Marginal cost per croissant}\\\\
&=\$3 - \$3.00\\\\
&=\$0.00\\\\
\text{Producer surplus from selling } 100 \text{croissants} &= 100 \times \text{Producer surplus per croissant}\\\\
&=100 \times \$0.00\\\\
&=\$0\end{align}$

Total producer surplus in the market is the sum of producer surplus ($PS$) for all firms willing to sell:

$\begin{align} \text{Total Producer Surplus} &=PS_A +PS_B+PS_C\\\\
&=\$75 + \$25 + \$0
&=\$100\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is the best definition of allocative efficiency?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When firms' total revenues are maximized
When consumers experience the maximum consumer surplus at the expense of producer surplus
When the marginal benefit of consumers equals the marginal cost of producers
When production occurs at the lowest possible cost and productivity is maximized
When a good's price is maximized in order to benefit producers

assessment_items.question_content:
**In a perfectly competitive market, which of the following best describes the price that will be the most efficient?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The price that maximizes total surplus
The price that maximizes consumer surplus
The price that maximizes producer surplus
The price that maximizes deadweight loss
Any price except the equilibrium price

assessment_items.question_content:
The demand for banana nut muffins is shown in this graph. 
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1dc78034f522efb43199257ac0c402954650c16a)

**What happens to consumer surplus if the price decreases from $\$8$ to $\$5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To calculate the change in consumer surplus, calculate the initial consumer surplus and the new consumer surplus, then find the difference between these values.

Consumer surplus when price is $\$8$ is $\$4$:

$\begin{align} \text{CS before} & = \dfrac{1}{2} \times  (\$10 - \$8) \times 4\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \$2 \times 4\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \$8\\\\
& = \$4 \end{align}$

Consumer surplus when price is $\$5$ is $\$25$:

$\begin{align} \text{CS after} & = \dfrac{1}{2} \times  (\$10 - \$5) \times 10\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \$5 \times 10\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \$50\\\\
& = \$25 \end{align}$

When price falls from $\$8$ to $\$5$ in this market, consumer suplus increases by $\$21$:

$\begin{align}\text{Change in consumer surplus } &= \$25 - \$4 & = \$21\end{align}$

**What area represents producer surplus in the graph shown here if this market is in equilibrium?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**What must be true about a perfectly competitive market in equilibrium that is allocatively efficient?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Total surplus is the highest it can be
Deadweight loss is the highest it can be
Producers have more surplus than consumers
Consumers have more surplus than producers
